questions.q: The complexity class P consists of languages that:
options: Can be recognized by a polynomial number of Turing machines
Can be decided by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time
Can be verified by a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time
Are polynomial-length strings only
questions.explanation: P (polynomial time) comprises decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time relative to input size. This includes practical problems like sorting and graph reachability. Option 2 is the standard definition. NP involves verification (option 3), and option 1 conflates number of machines with time complexity.

questions.q: The complexity class NP consists of languages where:
options: No polynomial algorithm is known
A certificate for membership can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic machine
A non-deterministic Turing machine can solve it in exponential time
The problem is 'not polynomial' and definitely requires exponential time
questions.explanation: NP (non-deterministic polynomial time) consists of problems where a proposed solution (certificate) can be checked in polynomial time. Example: SAT, where a truth assignment is the certificate. 'Not polynomial' (option 4) is a misreading of 'NP'. NP problems may or may not have polynomial solutions; P vs NP is open. Option 2 defines decidability, not NP.

options: It is in NP and every problem in NP reduces to it in polynomial time
It is the hardest problem in NP
It cannot be solved in polynomial time
It is in P and also in NP
questions.explanation: NP-complete requires two conditions: (1) membership in NP (verifiable in polynomial time), and (2) NP-hardness (all NP problems reduce to it polynomially). This makes NP-complete problems 'hardest' in NP in a technical sense. Option 2 is informal; option 3 conflates NP-completeness with proven exponential hardness; option 4 describes problems unlikely to exist (barring P = NP).

questions.q: If P = NP, which of the following would be true?
options: All problems would be solvable in polynomial time
The Halting Problem would become decidable
Every problem in NP could be solved (not merely verified) in polynomial time
PSPACE would equal P
questions.explanation: If P = NP, then every problem verifiable in polynomial time is also solvable in polynomial time. This would mean NP-complete problems like SAT have polynomial algorithms. Not all problems are in NP (option 1 overstates); the Halting Problem remains undecidable (option 2); PSPACE does not collapse to P (option 3). Option 2 is the precise consequence of P = NP.

questions.q: A polynomial reduction from problem A to problem B means:
options: A is easier than B
A can be converted to an instance of B in polynomial time such that the solution for B yields a solution for A
Every solution to A is also a solution to B
B requires more time to solve than A
questions.explanation: A polynomial reduction A ≤_p B uses a polynomial-time computable function to transform any instance of A into an instance of B, preserving yes/no answers. If B is solvable in polynomial time, then A is too. Reductions establish hardness relationships but do not directly compare difficulty. Option 1 is informal; option 3 conflates decision problems with solution preservation.

questions.q: The Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-complete. What does this imply?
options: SAT cannot be solved in polynomial time
SAT is in NP and any problem in NP reduces to SAT in polynomial time
SAT is the only NP-complete problem
SAT is decidable and verifiable in logarithmic time
questions.explanation: NP-completeness of SAT means SAT is in NP (truth assignments verify in polynomial time) and is NP-hard (all NP problems reduce to it polynomially). Option 0 is unproven conjecture; option 2 is false—there are many NP-complete problems; option 3 confuses decidability with complexity. The definition of NP-completeness is exactly option 1.

questions.q: PSPACE is the set of languages decidable by a Turing machine using:
options: Polynomial time and unlimited space
Polynomial space and unlimited time
Exponential space and exponential time
Linear space only
questions.explanation: PSPACE (polynomial space) comprises languages decidable in polynomial space, but time is unrestricted. We know P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE, but P = PSPACE is open. Option 0 bounds time (not PSPACE); option 2 is EXPSPACE; option 3 is linear space, a smaller class. PSPACE allows any polynomial amount of memory.

questions.q: Which statement correctly orders these complexity classes by inclusion?
options: P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE ⊆ EXPTIME
NP ⊆ P ⊆ PSPACE
PSPACE ⊆ P ⊆ NP
EXPTIME ⊆ PSPACE ⊆ NP ⊆ P
questions.explanation: The known hierarchy is P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE ⊆ EXPTIME (exponential time). These strict inclusions follow from simulation relationships between models and resource bounds. NP ⊆ P is unproven and unlikely; PSPACE ⊆ P is false; EXPTIME ⊆ PSPACE is false. Option 0 reflects current understanding of the landscape.

questions.q: The 3-SAT problem (satisfiability of boolean formulas in 3-CNF) is:
options: Decidable in polynomial time
NP-complete, meaning it is as hard as any problem in NP
In P but not in NP
Undecidable
questions.explanation: 3-SAT is a canonical NP-complete problem: membership is in NP (verify truth assignments in polynomial time), and NP-hardness is proven by reduction from general SAT (Cook's theorem). No polynomial-time algorithm for 3-SAT is known, and finding one would prove P = NP. It is decidable but hardness remains unresolved.

questions.q: If a problem is NP-hard, what can we conclude?
options: The problem is in NP
Every problem in NP reduces to it in polynomial time
The problem is polynomial-time solvable
The problem is undecidable
questions.explanation: NP-hardness means all NP problems polynomially reduce to it. NP-hardness does not require the problem to be in NP itself (option 0 is NP-completeness); NP-hard problems need not be solvable in polynomial time (option 2); undecidability is unrelated to NP-hardness (option 3). Option 1 is the defining property of NP-hardness.
